# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the term "Safety Integrity Level" (SIL) and name the standard that defines it.  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** is a discrete, four-tier classification (SIL 1, 2, 3, 4) used to specify the level of risk reduction required for a safety function. It is defined in the international standard **IEC 61508**. The higher the SIL, the lower the **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** that the safety function will fail to perform when needed. For industrial robots, emergency stop circuits typically require **SIL 3 or higher**.

---

### Question 2: State the standard that governs collaborative robot safety and list two of its key biomechanical limits.  `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Collaborative robot safety is governed by **ISO/TS 15066**. The two key biomechanical limits are:
> 1. **Quasi-static contact force** $\leq 140$ N (hand/finger)
> 2. **Maximum speed in collaborative mode** $\leq 250$ mm/s

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks):**  `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** [7 marks] Discuss the **risk assessment methodology** for an industrial robot workcell, including the formula and its interpretation.

**(b)** [7 marks] With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **functional safety architecture** of a typical industrial robot cell. List at least **four** safety devices and their role.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Need for Risk Assessment:** Industrial robots have large, unpredictable 3D motion envelopes. Each new installation is unique; therefore, generic compliance is not enough — a **case-by-case** risk assessment is mandatory under ISO 10218-2.

**Step 2 — Formula Definition:**

$$
R = S \times P \times E
$$

- $S$ (Severity of injury) — scale 1 to 5
- $P$ (Probability of occurrence) — scale 1 to 5
- $E$ (Frequency of exposure) — scale 1 to 5

**Step 3 — Risk Matrix Bands:**

| Range of $R$ | Risk Level | Action |
|---|---|---|
| 1 – 25 | Low | Accept, monitor |
| 26 – 50 | Medium | Reduce via controls |
| 51 – 75 | High | Mandatory reduction |
| 76 – 125 | Unacceptable | Halt, redesign |

**Step 4 — Hierarchy of Controls:** The corrective action follows the order:
**Elimination → Substitution → Engineering Controls → Administrative Controls → PPE**

**Step 5 — Example:** A spot-welding cobot may yield $R = 4 \times 2 \times 4 = 32$ (Medium). Mitigation = install a **force-limiting end-effector** and **enforce reduced speed 250 mm/s**.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Functional Safety Architecture Block Diagram (textual representation):**

```
[SAFETY INPUTS]                       [SAFETY LOGIC]            [SAFETY OUTPUTS]
─ Safety Fence/Interlock    ─┐
─ Light Curtain             ─┤
─ E-Stop Buttons            ─┼─▶ [SAFETY PLC] ─▶ [ROBOT CONTROLLER] ─▶ [STO + STOP CATS]
─ Safety Mat                ─┤        ▲                │
─ 3-Pos Enabling Device     ─┤        │                └─▶ [HMI Indicator]
─ Force/Torque Sensor (Cobot)─┘       │
                                     │
                              [LOCKOUT/TAGOUT]
```

**Four Safety Devices and Their Roles:**

| Device | Role |
|---|---|
| **Light Curtain** | Detects intrusion at the workcell perimeter; immediately triggers stop. |
| **E-Stop Button** | Hardwired, normally-closed, dual-channel stop; PL e / Cat 4. |
| **Safety Mat** | Detects operator stepping into forbidden zone; presence-based. |
| **3-Position Enabling Device** | Allows motion in **teach mode** only while held in mid-position. |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):**  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** [7 marks] Derive the **safety distance formula** for a perimeter guard. A robot stop time is 0.4 s, light curtain response time is 0.025 s, and the reach constant is 30 mm. Find the safety distance $S_d$ (assume approach speed $K = 1600$ mm/s).

**(b)** [7 marks] Explain the **Lockout/Tagout (LOTO) procedure** with all six steps. Why is "verify zero energy" the most critical step?

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Concept:** The safety distance ensures the operator's hand **cannot reach the hazard** before the robot has fully stopped.

**Step 2 — Derivation:** The hand travels at approach speed $K$ for the combined duration $(T_s + T_r)$. Therefore the hand's travel distance is:

$$
d_{hand} = K \times (T_s + T_r)
$$

The hand may also extend a depth $C$ into the protected field before detection. Hence:

$$
S_d = K \times (T_s + T_r) + C
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Values:**

Given $K = 1600$ mm/s, $T_s = 0.4$ s, $T_r = 0.025$ s, $C = 30$ mm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_d &= 1600 \times (0.4 + 0.025) + 30 \\
&= 1600 \times 0.425 + 30 \\
&= 680.0 + 30 \\
&= 710 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:** The light curtain must be placed at least **710 mm** from the nearest hazard point to guarantee operator safety.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Six LOTO Steps:**

1. **Notify** all affected employees that maintenance is starting.
2. **Identify** all energy sources (electrical, pneumatic, hydraulic, gravitational).
3. **Isolate** the energy source (open disconnect, close valve).
4. **Lockout** — apply a **personal padlock** to the energy-isolating device.
5. **Verify zero energy** — attempt normal start; check gauges, voltmeters, indicators.
6. **Release stored energy** — bleed air, drain hydraulics, lower suspended masses.

**Why Step 5 is the most critical:**

> Step 5 is the **physical proof** that the system is safe. It is the **only step that prevents the common fatal error** of trusting the lockout mechanically. Residual energy from capacitors, springs, or compressed air can still cause injury if not verified to be at zero. Skipping this step is a leading root cause in industrial accident reports.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Never confuse SIL with PL** — SIL is from IEC 61508, PL is from ISO 13849-1. Examiners deduct full marks for interchanging them.
> 2. **Always write the safety-distance formula with units** — losing 1 mark for writing $K$ without mm/s.
> 3. **Do not place PPE first in your hierarchy** — PPE is the **last** resort; it must come at the bottom of the Hierarchy of Controls.
> 4. **"Notify" is Step 1, not Step 6** in LOTO — the order is examinable.
> 5. **Forgetting the reach constant $C$** in safety-distance problems loses 2 marks in a 7-mark part.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core Standards:** ISO 10218-1 (Robot), ISO 10218-2 (System), ISO/TS 15066 (Cobots), ANSI/RIA R15.06, ISO 13849-1, IEC 61508.
- **Key Formula:** $S_d = K(T_s + T_r) + C$ with $K = 1600$ mm/s.
- **Cobot Speed Limit:** 250 mm/s during collaborative operation.
- **Quasi-static Force Limit:** 140 N (hand/finger).
- **Risk Equation:** $R = S \times P \times E$ — memorize the matrix bands (1–25, 26–50, 51–75, 76–125).
- **E-Stop Category:** PL e / Cat 4 / SIL 3.
- **LOTO has 6 steps** — "Notify → Identify → Isolate → Lockout → Verify → Release".
- **Hierarchy of Controls:** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE.
- **Interlocks** must be **dual-channel** for PL d or higher.
- **Three-position enabling device** is mandatory for **teach-mode** operation.
- **Safety mats** detect presence; **light curtains** detect intrusion; **laser scanners** detect approach — choose based on access pattern.
- **Verify zero energy** is the most critical LOTO step.
- **Force/Torque sensing** is the heart of cobot safety per ISO/TS 15066.
- **Performance Level vs SIL:** PL d ≈ SIL 2, PL e ≈ SIL 3 (approximate equivalence often quoted in KTU answers).
- **Don't forget:** Stored energy in **pneumatic accumulators** and **spring-loaded grippers** can be lethal even after electrical lockout.

topics.title: Safety Design for Industrial Robot Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT411 - PERUT411
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Safety Design for Industrial Robot Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Safety Design for Industrial Robot Systems** is the systematic, standards-driven engineering discipline concerned with identifying, evaluating, and mitigating physical, electrical, and operational hazards arising from the deployment of industrial robots and robot cells in manufacturing environments. It is governed primarily by the harmonized triad of:

- **ANSI/RIA R15.06-2012** (American National Standard for Industrial Robots and Robot Systems – Safety Requirements)
- **ISO 10218-1:2011** (Robots and robotic devices – Safety requirements for industrial robots – Part 1: Robots)
- **ISO 10218-2:2011** (Part 2: Robot systems and integration)
- **ISO/TS 15066:2016** (Technical Specification for Collaborative Robots)
- **ISO 13849-1:2015** (Safety of machinery – Safety-related parts of control systems)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Under the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned scheme, students must understand safety as an **integrated design philosophy** — not an add-on. Safety is realized through a **three-step methodology**: Inherent Safe Design → Safeguarding → Complementary Protective Measures → Information for Use.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Highway Intersection" Model

Imagine a high-speed automated warehouse where robotic forklifts zoom through narrow aisles at 2 m/s. Pedestrians (human workers) need to cross. How do we prevent collisions?

- **Inherent design**: Lower the forklift's top speed to 0.5 m/s in pedestrian zones (Power & Force Limiting).
- **Safeguards**: Install motion sensors that slow the forklift when a human is within 2 m (Speed & Separation Monitoring).
- **Stop signs**: Emergency stop buttons that halt the robot within 0.3 s (Safety-rated Stop).
- **Crosswalk lights**: Visual/audible warnings before the robot moves (Complementary measures).

This is exactly the layered "**Hierarchy of Controls**" used in robot safety design. Each layer independently and collectively reduces the residual risk to an acceptable threshold defined by the standards.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical safety design constants you must memorize for KTU examinations:**
>
> - **Hand approach speed (K):** 1600 mm/s (per ISO 13855, parallel approach)
> - **Whole-body approach speed:** 2000 mm/s
> - **Maximum transient power (collaborative):** 80 W
> - **Maximum quasi-static power (collaborative):** 20 W
> - **Maximum collaborative robot speed:** 250 mm/s
> - **Maximum clamping force on human body:** 150 N (ISO/TS 15066, quasi-static)
> - **Maximum pressure (clamping):** 5 × 10⁴ N/m² (5 bar)
> - **Safety integrity threshold PFHₐ:** 10⁻⁷ to 10⁻⁸ per hour (PL d / SIL 2 minimum for most robot E-stops)
> - **Average ambient temperature reference:** 25 °C (for stop time calculations)
> - **Gravitational acceleration (g):** 9.81 m/s²

### 1.4 The Three Classes of Robot Hazards

| Hazard Class | Typical Robot Scenario | Typical Injury Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical (Kinematic)** | Unguarded pinch point between arm and fixture | Crushing, shearing, entanglement |
| **Electrical** | Servo drive cabinet opened for maintenance | Arc flash, electric shock |
| **Pneumatic / Hydraulic** | End-effactor gripper actuated during teach | Skin injection, whiplash |
| **Thermal** | Spot-welding robot after continuous duty | Burns |
| **Ergonomic / Cognitive** | Operator misjudges robot trajectory | Startle, panic-induced fall |
| **Stray Process Hazards** | Laser cutting, paint spraying | Eye damage, chemical exposure |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Risk Assessment Ladder (ISO 12100 Framework)

The design of any industrial robot cell **must** follow this structured risk reduction process, repeated iteratively until residual risk is acceptable.

1. **Hazard Identification** — Enumerate every conceivable hazard (mechanical, electrical, ergonomic, etc.) for the **full lifecycle**: installation, commissioning, programming, operation, maintenance, decommissioning.
2. **Risk Estimation** — For each hazard, assign:
   - **Severity of injury (S):** S1 = slight, S2 = serious (irreversible, death)
   - **Frequency of exposure (F):** F1 = seldom, F2 = often
   - **Possibility of avoidance (P):** P1 = possible, P2 = impossible
3. **Risk Evaluation** — Compute Risk Score:

$$
\text{Risk Score} = S \times F \times P
$$

   - Score ≤ 4 → Acceptable
   - 5 ≤ Score ≤ 10 → ALARP (As Low As Reasonably Practicable)
   - Score > 10 → Mandatory reduction before deployment

4. **Risk Reduction** — Apply controls in **strict order** (hierarchy of controls):
   - **Inherent safe design** (remove hazard, reduce energy)
   - **Safeguarding** (guards, interlocks, two-hand controls)
   - **Complementary measures** (emergency stops, warnings)
   - **Information for use** (signage, training, manuals)

5. **Verification & Validation** — Measure stopping time, calculate safety distance, certify against PL/SIL.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Performance Level (**PL**) and Safety Integrity Level (**SIL**) are *not* the same. PL is from ISO 13849 (probability-based, hardware-driven), SIL is from IEC 61508 (probability + software + systematic capability). For robot safety, **PL d with Category 3** is the typical minimum for protective stops.

### 2.2 Functional Safety — Linking Performance Level to PFHₐ

The **Probability of dangerous Failure per Hour (PFHₐ)** defines the reliability of a safety function:

$$
\text{PFH}_{d} = \sum \lambda_{D}
$$

where $\lambda_{D}$ is the dangerous failure rate of each channel (in failures/hour). The five Performance Levels from **PL a** to **PL e** are:

| Performance Level | PFHₐ (per hour) | Average Probability of Dangerous Failure |
|---|---|---|
| **PL a** | 10⁻⁵ ≤ PFHₐ < 10⁻⁴ | ≥ 10⁻¹ |
| **PL b** | 10⁻⁶ ≤ PFHₐ < 10⁻⁵ | 10⁻² to 10⁻¹ |
| **PL c** | 10⁻⁶ ≤ PFHₐ < 10⁻⁶ | 10⁻³ to 10⁻² |
| **PL d** | 10⁻⁷ ≤ PFHₐ < 10⁻⁶ | 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻³ |
| **PL e** | 4 × 10⁻⁹ ≤ PFHₐ < 10⁻⁷ | 10⁻⁵ to 10⁻⁴ |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Standard Reference | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S = (K \times T) + C$ | Minimum safety distance (ISO 13855) | mm |
| 2 | $K = 1600$ | Hand approach speed | mm/s |
| 3 | $K = 2000$ | Whole-body approach speed | mm/s |
| 4 | $T = t_{1} + t_{2}$ | Total stopping time = reaction time + braking time | s |
| 5 | $C = 8 \times (d - 14)$ | Additional distance for opt. devices (d in mm) | mm |
| 6 | $\text{Risk Score} = S \times F \times P$ | Risk estimation per ISO 12100 | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $\text{PFH}_{d} = \sum \lambda_{D}$ | Probability of dangerous failure per hour | hr⁻¹ |
| 8 | $F_{\text{brake}} = \mu \cdot N$ | Clamping/contact force at stop | N |
| 9 | $v_{\max} = 250$ | Max collaborative robot speed | mm/s |
| 10 | $P_{\text{quasi-static}} \le 20$ | Max power during sustained contact | W |
| 11 | $P_{\text{transient}} \le 80$ | Max power during dynamic contact | W |
| 12 | $F_{\max,\text{clamp}} = 150$ | Quasi-static clamping force limit | N |
| 13 | $a = \frac{v_f^2 - v_i^2}{2 \cdot d}$ | Robot deceleration during protective stop | m/s² |
| 14 | $E_{k} = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Kinetic energy of moving end-effector | J |
| 15 | $s_{\text{brake}} = v_i \cdot t_r + \tfrac{1}{2} a t_r^2$ | Stopping distance under constant decel | m |

> [!WARNING]
> **In equation (5) above**, when using the table inside this KTU formula sheet, the conditional **if d > 14 mm** must be applied; for electro-sensitive devices with resolution ≤ 14 mm, $C = 0$ is the permitted minimum. KTU examiners frequently award 1 mark solely for this distinction.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In automotive body-in-white welding cells, payload manipulators weighing **1200 kg** with arm tips moving at **2.5 m/s** carry a kinetic energy in excess of **3750 J** — enough to fracture a human femur. Safety design here is non-negotiable. The same physics principles drive the safety envelopes in semiconductor cleanrooms, pharmaceutical compounding cells, and aerospace CNC tending cells.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation 1 — Safety Distance for an Electro-Sensitive Protective Device (ESPE)

This is the single most important derivation in the entire module and the most frequently asked KTU 14-mark question.

> **Problem Statement:** A light curtain with resolution $d = 30$ mm guards a palletizing robot. The total system stopping time $T = 0.42$ s. Compute the minimum mounting distance $S$ such that an operator's hand reaching into the danger zone cannot reach the hazard before the robot stops.

**Step 1 — Recall the governing standard equation (ISO 13855):**

$$
S = (K \times T) + C
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the hand approach speed (ISO 13855 default, parallel approach):**

$$
K = 1600 \text{ mm/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the total stopping time given:**

$$
T = 0.42 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the kinematic component of the safety distance:**

$$
K \times T = 1600 \times 0.42
$$

$$
K \times T = 672 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the C-correction for an opt. device with resolution d = 30 mm:**

$$
C = 8 \times (d - 14)
$$

$$
C = 8 \times (30 - 14)
$$

$$
C = 8 \times 16
$$

$$
C = 128 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6 — Sum the two components to obtain the minimum safety distance:**

$$
S = 672 + 128
$$

$$
\boxed{S = 800 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 7 — Add a positional tolerance (typically +50 mm in practice per ISO 13855):**

$$
S_{\text{practical}} = S + 50 = 850 \text{ mm}
$$

> **Physical interpretation:** The light curtain must be mounted at least 800 mm away from the pinch point. Otherwise, a hand crossing at 1600 mm/s reaches the hazard in 0.42 s — the same time the robot needs to stop — and a collision becomes inevitable.

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation 2 — Stopping Time Decomposition with Braking Dynamics

Given a 6-axis articulated robot with payload $m = 75$ kg reaching a final velocity $v_i = 2.0$ m/s, decelerating uniformly to $v_f = 0$ over a braking distance $d = 0.4$ m, compute the robot's required deceleration, the time to stop, and the resulting energy dissipation.

**Step 1 — Compute deceleration using the kinematic equation $v_f^2 = v_i^2 - 2 a d$:**

$$
0 = (2.0)^2 - 2 \times a \times 0.4
$$

$$
0 = 4.0 - 0.8 a
$$

$$
0.8 a = 4.0
$$

$$
\boxed{a = 5.0 \text{ m/s}^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the kinematic stopping time $t_r$ using $v_f = v_i - a t_r$:**

$$
0 = 2.0 - 5.0 \times t_r
$$

$$
t_r = \frac{2.0}{5.0}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_r = 0.4 \text{ s}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the kinetic energy to be dissipated (must be absorbed by the brake, not the human body):**

$$
E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m v_i^2
$$

$$
E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 75 \times (2.0)^2
$$

$$
E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 75 \times 4.0
$$

$$
\boxed{E_k = 150 \text{ J}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the equivalent stopping force for risk mapping:**

$$
F_{\text{stop}} = m \times a
$$

$$
F_{\text{stop}} = 75 \times 5.0
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{\text{stop}} = 375 \text{ N}}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with the ISO/TS 15066 quasi-static contact limit of 150 N:**

$$
\frac{F_{\text{stop}}}{F_{\max}} = \frac{375}{150} = 2.5
$$

This robot's protective stop **exceeds** the 150 N quasi-static contact threshold by a factor of 2.5. This means it **cannot** be deployed as a collaborative robot in Power & Force Limiting (PFL) mode without additional safety-rated speed reduction to 250 mm/s or below.

> **Engineering implication:** The integrator must either install **safeguarding (light curtain + safety mat)** to prevent human-robot contact entirely, or use **Speed & Separation Monitoring (SSM)** to keep the human outside the robot's reach envelope during high-speed moves.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Collaborative Robot Operation Modes

| Operation Mode | Standard Reference | Maximum Speed | Contact Energy Limit | Mandatory Safety Function |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Safety-Rated Monitored Stop (SRMS)** | ISO 10218-1 §5.10 | 0 mm/s (robot must be at standstill) | N/A (no contact) | Stop Category 2 + STO |
| **Hand Guiding** | ISO 10218-1 §5.11 | 250 mm/s | 80 W transient | Enabling device + PL d |
| **Speed & Separation Monitoring (SSM)** | ISO 10218-1 §5.12 | Variable, distance-dependent | Distance-based | Safety-rated soft axis limits |
| **Power & Force Limiting (PFL)** | ISO/TS 15066 | 250 mm/s | 80 W / 20 W | Force/torque sensing + PL d Cat 3 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Robot Safety Zones (Spatial Topology)

```mermaid
graph TB
    HAZARD[Robot End Effector Pinch Point]
    OPERATE[Operating Space - Robot reachable volume]
    RESTRICT[Restricted Space - Limited by software limits]
    SAFE[Safeguarded Space - Bounded by physical safeguards]
    COLL[Collaborative Workspace - Shared with human]
    HUMAN[Human Operator]
    GUARD[Safety Light Curtain / Safety Mat]
    ESTOP[Emergency Stop Hardware]
    PLC[Safety PLC Monitoring I-O]

    HAZARD --> OPERATE
    OPERATE --> RESTRICT
    RESTRICT --> SAFE
    SAFE --> COLL
    COLL --> HUMAN

    GUARD -.protects.-> HUMAN
    ESTOP -.halts.-> SAFE
    PLC -.monitors.-> GUARD
    PLC -.monitors.-> ESTOP
    PLC -.triggers.-> RESTRICT
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Risk Assessment Flow (ISO 12100)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Machine Limits] --> B[Identify Hazards]
    B --> C[Estimate Risk Score S x F x P]
    C --> D{Score Acceptable?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Document Residual Risk]
    D -- No --> F[Apply Inherent Safe Design]
    F --> G[Apply Safeguarding]
    G --> H[Apply Complementary Measures]
    H --> I[Information for Use]
    I --> J[Verify and Validate]
    J --> C
    E --> K[Deploy to Production]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Functional Safety Architecture (Block-Level Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
graph LR
    INPUT1[Safety Light Curtain Input 1] --> LOGIC
    INPUT2[Safety Mat Input 2] --> LOGIC
    INPUT3[Emergency Stop Pushbutton] --> LOGIC
    INPUT4[Two-Hand Control Device] --> LOGIC
    LOGIC[Safety PLC with Redundant Processor PL d Category 3] --> OUT1
    LOGIC --> OUT2
    OUT1[Contactors Cutting Servo Drive] --> ROBOT
    OUT2[Safety Relay Energised] --> ROBOT
    ROBOT[Industrial Robot Drive Train]
    LOGIC --> HMI[Safety Status HMI Display]
    LOGIC --> LOG[Event Logger with Time Stamp]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture Explanation

The diagram above is **not** a wiring schematic. It is a functional safety block diagram showing how multiple input safety devices (light curtain, safety mat, E-stop, two-hand control) all funnel into a single safety PLC, which executes the safety logic and issues hardwired outputs to the robot's contactors. Every path is redundant (dual-channel) to satisfy PL d / Category 3. The HMI is **not** part of the safety chain — it is for human-readable status only.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Exam Insight:** A single-channel E-stop wired to the safety PLC input is **Category 1 (B1 or B2)** — it does **not** meet PL d. A **dual-channel, cross-monitored** E-stop with redundant contactors is the minimum for PL d Cat 3.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "Performance Level (PL)" as per ISO 13849-1 and list the five PL categories with their corresponding PFHₐ ranges.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Performance Level (PL) is a discrete level used to specify the ability of safety-related parts of a control system to perform a safety function under foreseeable conditions. There are five levels from PL a to PL e.

$$
\text{PFH}_{d} \text{ range mapping:}

$$

- **PL a:** $10^{-5} \le \text{PFH}_{d} < 10^{-4}$ (1 mark)
- **PL b:** $10^{-6} \le \text{PFH}_{d} < 10^{-5}$ (0.5 marks)
- **PL c:** $10^{-6} \le \text{PFH}_{d} < 10^{-6}$ (0.5 marks)
- **PL d:** $10^{-7} \le \text{PFH}_{d} < 10^{-6}$ (0.5 marks)
- **PL e:** $4 \times 10^{-9} \le \text{PFH}_{d} < 10^{-7}$ (0.5 marks)

(1 mark for the definition and reference to ISO 13849-1)

#### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between "Safeguarding" and "Complementary Protective Measures" as applied to industrial robot safety.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Safeguarding | Complementary Protective Measure |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Measures to protect persons from hazards that cannot be fully eliminated by design | Additional protective measures used in conjunction with safeguarding |
| **Examples** | Guards, interlocks, light curtains, two-hand controls, safety mats (2 marks) | Emergency stop, audible/visual warnings, signs, instructions for use (1 mark) |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the **risk assessment methodology** as outlined in **ISO 12100** for an industrial robot system. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (a):**

ISO 12100 specifies a systematic process for risk assessment consisting of the following steps:

1. **Determination of machine limits** (intended use, foreseeable misuse, spatial limits, environmental limits). (1 mark)
2. **Hazard identification** — enumerate all hazards (mechanical, electrical, thermal, ergonomic) across the lifecycle: installation, commissioning, operation, maintenance, decommissioning. (1 mark)
3. **Risk estimation** — for each hazard, assess:
   - Severity of injury (S1 / S2)
   - Frequency of exposure (F1 / F2)
   - Possibility of avoidance (P1 / P2)
   - Calculate the risk score: $R = S \times F \times P$ (1 mark)
4. **Risk evaluation** — determine if residual risk is acceptable. (0.5 mark)
5. **Risk reduction** — apply the hierarchy of controls:
   - Inherent safe design
   - Safeguarding
   - Complementary measures
   - Information for use (2 marks)
6. **Verification** — confirm that protective measures are effective and meet the required PL/SIL. (1 mark)
7. **Iteration** — if risk is still unacceptable, return to step 5. (0.5 mark)

**Q. (b)** A light curtain with **resolution d = 25 mm** is used to safeguard a robot cell. The total system stopping time (response + braking) is **T = 0.55 s**. Compute the minimum safety distance and the practical mounting distance with a 50 mm positional tolerance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Recall ISO 13855 safety distance equation:**

$$
S = (K \times T) + C
$$

(1 mark)

**Step 2 — Substitute K for hand approach speed:**

$$
K = 1600 \text{ mm/s}
$$

(0.5 mark)

**Step 3 — Compute the kinematic component:**

$$
K \times T = 1600 \times 0.55 = 880 \text{ mm}
$$

(1 mark)

**Step 4 — Compute the C-correction:**

$$
C = 8 \times (d - 14) = 8 \times (25 - 14) = 8 \times 11 = 88 \text{ mm}
$$

(2 marks)

**Step 5 — Sum to get S:**

$$
S = 880 + 88 = 968 \text{ mm}
$$

(1.5 marks)

**Step 6 — Add the practical tolerance:**

$$
S_{\text{practical}} = 968 + 50 = 1018 \text{ mm}
$$

(1 mark)

**Result:** The light curtain must be mounted at least 968 mm from the hazard (1018 mm in practice).

#### Question B (14 Marks Alternative) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q. (a)** Describe the **four collaborative robot operation modes** as per ISO 10218-1 and ISO/TS 15066, with their respective safety constraints. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (a):**

1. **Safety-Rated Monitored Stop (SRMS):** Robot must be at standstill before human enters collaborative workspace. Implemented via STO (Safe Torque Off). **(1.5 marks)**
2. **Hand Guiding:** Operator physically leads the robot via an enabling device on the end-effector. Max speed **250 mm/s**, max transient power **80 W**. **(1.5 marks)**
3. **Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM):** Robot speed is dynamically adjusted to maintain a protective separation distance from the human. **PL d** minimum. **(2 marks)**
4. **Power and Force Limiting (PFL):** Robot is intrinsically designed so that contact forces/powers cannot exceed biomechanical thresholds: **80 W transient, 20 W quasi-static, 150 N clamping force, 5 × 10⁴ N/m² pressure**. **(2 marks)**

**Q. (b)** A SCARA robot carries a payload of **m = 12 kg** at a TCP speed of **v = 1.8 m/s**. The protective stop decelerates it uniformly over **d = 0.3 m**.
Compute:
(i) The deceleration
(ii) The stopping time
(iii) The kinetic energy absorbed by the brake
(iv) Whether this robot can be deployed in PFL collaborative mode. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Deceleration (1.5 marks):**

$$
v_f^2 = v_i^2 - 2 a d
$$

$$
0 = (1.8)^2 - 2 \times a \times 0.3
$$

$$
a = \frac{3.24}{0.6} = 5.4 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

**(ii) Stopping time (1.5 marks):**

$$
t_r = \frac{v_i}{a} = \frac{1.8}{5.4} = 0.333 \text{ s}
$$

**(iii) Kinetic energy (1.5 marks):**

$$
E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 12 \times (1.8)^2 = 6 \times 3.24 = 19.44 \text{ J}
$$

**(iv) Collaborative feasibility (2.5 marks):**

The energy of 19.44 J is **below** the 80 W transient power limit (assuming a contact time of ≈0.5 s: $80 \times 0.5 = 40$ J, 19.44 J < 40 J). However, the deceleration of 5.4 m/s² is acceptable for collaborative work (well within biomechanical thresholds of ~50 m/s² head acceleration). The stopping time of 0.333 s is also under the 0.5 s threshold. **Conclusion:** This robot **can** be deployed in PFL collaborative mode provided the speed is capped at 250 mm/s during human-robot interaction phases.

---

### 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks on Robot Safety Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the C-correction**: Writing $S = K \times T$ alone yields zero marks on the C-component.
> 2. **Mixing up K = 1600 vs 2000 mm/s**: Hand approach vs whole-body. Examiners award partial credit only if explicitly stated.
> 3. **Confusing Stop Categories**: Stop Category 0 is uncontrolled (immediate power removal), Stop Category 1 is controlled stop with delayed power removal, Stop Category 2 is monitored stop (power retained). Mixing these up costs 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting units in PFHₐ**: Always write "per hour" or "hr⁻¹" — units matter.
> 5. **Single-channel E-stop design** in answers — examiners expect dual-channel + cross-monitoring for PL d.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Three governing standards:** ISO 10218-1, ISO 10218-2, ISO/TS 15066.
- **Risk Score formula:** $R = S \times F \times P$ (max = 8 in old ISO, max = 1000+ in some scales — read the question carefully).
- **Safety distance formula:** $S = K \cdot T + C$, with $K = 1600$ mm/s and $C = 8(d - 14)$ for $d > 14$ mm.
- **Hierarchy of controls:** Inherent → Safeguarding → Complementary → Information.
- **PL d minimum** for protective stops on industrial robots; **PFHₐ ≤ 10⁻⁶ per hour**.
- **Collaborative robot thresholds:** 80 W transient, 20 W quasi-static, 150 N clamping force, 250 mm/s max speed, 5 × 10⁴ N/m² pressure.
- **Five PL levels** mapped to PFHₐ ranges — memorize cold.
- **Stop Categories:** 0 (uncontrolled, immediate), 1 (controlled, immediate stop with power cut at end), 2 (monitored, power retained — must achieve standstill and monitor).
- **Spatial zones:** Operating → Restricted → Safeguarded → Collaborative.
- **Lockout/Tagout (LOTO)** is mandatory before any maintenance.
- **E-stop reset** must require deliberate human action — never auto-reset.
- **Light curtain resolution** determines C-correction. A 14 mm resolution is the minimum that allows $C = 0$.
- **Two-hand control** devices must be enforced within **0.5 s** synchronism (per ISO 13851).
- **Biomechanical limits** per ISO/TS 15066 must be checked at all anticipated contact regions (head, neck, chest, hand).
- **Risk re-assessment** is mandatory after every modification to the robot cell — this is often a 3-mark Part A question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT411 - PERUT411
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Connectors for Electronic Equipment - Safety of Machinery

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Connectors for Electronic Equipment — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** An **electrical connector** is a passive electromechanical device that provides a separable electrical connection between two active devices (sub-systems) without unacceptable signal distortion or power loss. Connectors are characterized by standardized **mating geometries**, **contact materials**, **current ratings**, **voltage ratings**, **mating cycles**, and **ingress protection (IP) classes** as defined by IEC 60603, IEC 61076, and MIL-STD-1553 series.

In the context of electronic equipment manufacturing, connectors are the **physical interface nodes** of every printed circuit board assembly (PCBA), cable harness, and panel-mount module. They govern:
- **Signal integrity** (impedance, crosstalk, bandwidth)
- **Power distribution** (current density, contact resistance)
- **Mechanical durability** (mating cycles, retention force)
- **Environmental resilience** (IP rating, operating temperature)

## 1.2 Safety of Machinery — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** **Machinery Safety** refers to the integrated set of design measures, protective devices, and operational procedures that reduce the risk arising from the use of machinery to an acceptable level, in conformance with the harmonized standard **ISO 12100:2010** and the **Machinery Directive 2006/42/EC**. It encompasses hazard identification, risk assessment, risk reduction, and validation of the safety-related control system (SRP/CS) per **ISO 13849-1** and **IEC 62061**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### Connector Analogy — The "Universal Power Adapter"
Think of a connector as the **mating socket on a modular universal power strip**: just as a 3-pin Indian plug can only enter a 3-pin socket (preventing wrong wiring), connectors enforce **geometric keying** so that only compatible subsystems connect. A USB-C connector, for instance, has 24 gold-plated contacts inside a reversible shell — engineered for **10,000 mating cycles** and a **contact resistance ≤ 30 mΩ**. The reverse analogy: a **connector failure** in a CNC machine is like a loose wall socket that arcs when current is drawn — it can cause downtime, data loss, or fire.

### Safety of Machinery Analogy — The "Smart Elevator Door"
Modern elevators are a perfect safety-of-machinery system: a **light curtain** detects a finger in the door path (perception), the **safety relay** interprets the signal (logic), and the **door interlock** prevents motion (action). If any one of these three fails, the elevator does not move. This is the **three-channel fail-safe architecture** mandated by ISO 13849-1. In CNC, robotic, and automated machinery, the same logic prevents human-machine collisions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Connector Specs (Industry Standard Values):**
> - **Mating cycles:** 100 (low-end) → 10,000 (USB, MIL-spec) → 50,000 (high-end test sockets)
> - **Contact resistance:** **< 5 mΩ** (power) to **< 30 mΩ** (signal) — measured per **IEC 60512-2-1**
> - **Insulation resistance:** **> 1000 MΩ** at **500 V DC**
> - **Dielectric Withstanding Voltage (DWV):** **1000 V AC** for 1 minute typical
> - **Operating temperature:** **−55 °C to +125 °C** (industrial grade)
> - **IP Rating (water/dust):** IP20 (indoor) → IP67 (outdoor/submersible) → IP69K (high-pressure hot washdown)

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Is KTU-High-Yield

| KTU Application Area | Connector Type | Safety Standard |
|---|---|---|
| CNC Machines (Fanuc, Siemens) | M12/M23 circular, RJ45 | ISO 13849-1 PL d |
| Industrial Robots (KUKA, ABB) | Harting Han® heavy-duty | IEC 62061 SIL 2 |
| PLC Panels (Allen-Bradley) | D-sub, Phoenix Contact | ISO 12100 |
| Medical Electronics | IEC 60601-1 rated | IEC 60601-1 |
| Automotive ECUs | FAKRA, HSD, MX150 | ISO 26262 (ASIL) |

---

## 1.5 Classification of Connectors — Quick Reference Matrix

| Class | Examples | Pin Count | Current | Mating Cycles | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Board-to-Board** | BTB mezzanine, BTBMezzo, BGA sockets | 4 – 2000 | 0.5 A | 30 – 100 | PCB stacking |
| **Wire-to-Board** | JST-XH, PH, KF251; AMP MTA-100 | 1 – 40 | 1 – 5 A | 30 | Internal harness |
| **Rectangular (D-sub)** | DB9, DB15, DB25, HD15 (VGA), SCSI | 9 – 78 | 5 A | 500 | Legacy PC I/O |
| **Circular (M-series)** | M5, M8, M12 (A, B, D, X, L, T-coded) | 2 – 17 | 4 – 16 A | 100 – 500 | Sensor/actuator |
| **Heavy-Duty (Han®)** | Harting Han 6B, 10B, 24B | 6 – 216 | 16 – 200 A | 500 | Industrial machinery |
| **Coaxial / RF** | BNC, SMA, SMB, N-type, FAKRA | 1 | n/a (50 Ω/75 Ω) | 500 | RF signal |
| **Fiber-Optic** | LC, SC, ST, MPO/MTP | 1 – 72 fibers | n/a | 500 – 1000 | High-BW data |
| **Modular (RJ)** | RJ11, RJ45, RJ50 | 4/8/10 | 1.5 A | 750 | Ethernet/telecom |
| **Power (IEC 60320)** | C13/C14, C19/C20, CEE 7/7 | 2 – 5 | 10 – 32 A | 1000 | AC mains |
| **Edge / Card-Edge** | PCI, PCIe, ISA, AGP, DIMM | 7 – 2000+ | 0.5 – 5 A | 50 | Motherboard slots |
| **IDC (Insulation Displacement)** | Ribbon cable (2.54 mm pitch) | 10 – 64 | 1 A | 25 | Parallel buses |
| **USB Series** | USB-A, B, C, Micro-B | 4 – 24 | 0.5 – 5 A | 10,000 | Consumer PC/peripheral |
| **HDMI / DisplayPort** | HDMI-A, C, D; DP, Mini-DP | 19 / 20 | 0.5 A | 10,000 | Audio/Video |

---

## 1.6 Safety of Machinery — Standard Pyramid

```
                  ┌─────────────────────────┐
                  │   ISO 12100 : 2010      │  ← Type-A (Basic)
                  │   Risk Assessment       │
                  └────────────┬────────────┘
                ┌──────────────┴──────────────┐
                │  ISO 13849-1 / IEC 62061    │  ← Type-B (Group)
                │  PL / SIL Determination     │
                └──────────────┬──────────────┘
         ┌─────────────────────┴─────────────────────┐
         │  Type-C: EN 60204-1, EN 61800-5-2         │  ← Product
         │  Machine-specific C-standards             │
         └─────────────────────┬─────────────────────┘
                  ┌────────────┴────────────┐
                  │   Validation, Test,     │  ← Verification
                  │   Commissioning         │
                  └─────────────────────────┘
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Anchor (Use This Mnemonic):** **"I-PV-RV"** = **Identify hazards → Pinpoint PL/SIL → Validate by test & Re-Verify**.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an Electronic Connector

A connector is composed of **five engineered sub-assemblies**:

1. **Contact (Pin/Socket)** — The current-carrying element. Material selection is **critical**:
   - Base metal: **Phosphor bronze (C5191)**, **Beryllium copper (C17200)**, or **Brass (C36000)**
   - Plating: **Gold over nickel (Au/Ni)** for low resistance, **Tin (Sn)** for cost-sensitive applications
   - Gold thickness: **0.4 µm (flash)** → **1.27 µm (standard)** → **2.54 µm (MIL-grade)**

2. **Insulator / Housing (Plastic Body)** — Holds contacts in precise pitch. Material: **LCP (Liquid Crystal Polymer)**, **PBT (Polybutylene Terephthalate)**, **Nylon 66** with **UL94 V-0** flame rating.

3. **Shell / Hood** — Provides **EMI/RFI shielding** (metal) or **mechanical ruggedness** (plastic + metalized).

4. **Strain Relief / Backshell** — Prevents wire tension from being transferred to the crimp joint.

5. **Latching Mechanism** — Threaded, bayonet, push-pull, or friction lock.

## 2.2 Connector Selection — Engineering Logic Flow

**Step 1 — Electrical Requirements:**
- Number of circuits: $N_{ckt}$
- Continuous current per contact: $I_{cont}$ (A)
- Working voltage: $V_{work}$ (V AC/DC)
- Frequency / data rate: $f_{sig}$ (Hz) or $B$ (bit/s)

**Step 2 — Mechanical Requirements:**
- Mating cycles: $N_{mate}$
- Engagement force per contact: $F_{e}$ (N) → typically **0.3 N to 5 N**
- Vibration profile: per **IEC 60068-2-6** (typically **5 g, 10–500 Hz**)
- Shock: per **IEC 60068-2-27** (typically **30 g, 11 ms**)

**Step 3 — Environmental Requirements:**
- IP rating required (IP20 → IP69K)
- Operating temperature: $T_{min}, T_{max}$
- Chemical exposure, UV, ozone

**Step 4 — Standards Compliance:**
- UL, CE, CSA, TUV, RoHS, REACH, IPC-A-610 (assembly quality class 1/2/3)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "How & Why" It Matters:** A wrong connector choice (e.g., gold flash in a sulfur-rich paper-mill environment) leads to **"gold creep" and contact corrosion** within 6 months, causing intermittent signal loss. This is the #1 field-failure cause in industrial connectors.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Connector Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — MEMORIZE FOR ESE**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Contact resistance** | $R_c = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L}{A}$ | mΩ | $\rho$ = resistivity (Cu = 1.68×10⁻⁸ Ω·m) |
| 2 | **Current density** | $J = \dfrac{I_{cont}}{A_{contact}}$ | A/mm² | Typical limit: **< 7 A/mm²** for tin-plated |
| 3 | **Voltage derating curve** | $V_{rated}(alt) = V_{rated}(sea) \times \left[1 - \dfrac{H - H_0}{k}\right]$ | V | $H$ = altitude in m, $k$ ≈ 7000 m |
| 4 | **Insulation resistance** | $R_{ins} = \dfrac{V_{test}}{I_{leak}}$ | MΩ | Test per IEC 60512-3-1 at **500 V DC** |
| 5 | **Capacitance between contacts** | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | pF | Determines high-freq rolloff |
| 6 | **Characteristic impedance (coax)** | $Z_0 = \dfrac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \ln\left(\dfrac{D}{d}\right)$ | Ω | 50 Ω (BNC) or 75 Ω (video) |
| 7 | **Skin depth (RF)** | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\rho}{\omega \mu_0}}$ | µm | $\omega = 2\pi f$ |
| 8 | **Mean Time Between Failures** | $MTBF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | hours | $\lambda$ = failure rate (FIT) |
| 9 | **Cumulative mating force** | $F_{total} = N_{ckt} \times F_e \times 1.3$ | N | 30 % safety margin |
| 10 | **Contact rating (IEC 60512)** | $I_{max} = 0.5 \sqrt{T_{max} - 25 \text{ °C}}$ | A | Empirical derating for > 8 contacts bundled |
| 11 | **De-rating factor (multi-pin)** | $k_D = 1 - 0.04 \times (N_{ckt} - 8)$ | – | For $N_{ckt} > 8$ |
| 12 | **PL → SIL mapping** | PL a → SIL 1; PL d → SIL 2; PL e → SIL 3 | – | Per EN ISO 13849-1 / IEC 61508 |
| 13 | **PFHd (probability of dangerous failure)** | $PFH_d = \sum \lambda_{DU}$ per hour | FIT | For PL d, $PFH_d$ must be **< 10⁻⁷ /h** |
| 14 | **Risk score (EN ISO 12100)** | $R = S \times F \times P$ | – | $S$ = severity, $F$ = frequency, $P$ = possibility of avoidance |
| 15 | **Stop-time safety distance** | $S = v \times T_s + C$ | m | $v$ = approach speed, $T_s$ = total stop time |

---

## 2.4 Safety of Machinery — Hierarchical Risk Reduction

Per **ISO 12100:2010**, the three-step method (in order of preference):

| Step | Measure | Example | Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Inherent Safe Design** | Reduce motor power, use rounded edges, limit forces | **Highest** |
| 2 | **Safeguarding (Engineering Control)** | Light curtain, safety mat, interlocked guard, two-hand control | **Medium** |
| 3 | **Information for Use (PPE/Procedures)** | Warning labels, training, lockout-tagout | **Lowest** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Trap:** Students often list PPE *first* in their answers. **PPE is ALWAYS the last resort** in the ISO 12100 hierarchy. Examiners deduct 2–3 marks for this.

---

## 2.5 Performance Level (PL) & Safety Integrity Level (SIL) — Decoding Table

| PL | Avg PFH_d (per hour) | SIL (IEC 61508) | Avg PFH_d (SIL) | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **a** | 10⁻⁵ to < 10⁻⁴ | – | – | Minor, manual reset |
| **b** | 10⁻⁶ to < 10⁻⁵ | **1** | 10⁻⁶ to < 10⁻⁵ | Light guard, simple interlock |
| **c** | 10⁻⁷ to < 10⁻⁶ | **1** | – | Guard door with monitoring |
| **d** | 10⁻⁸ to < 10⁻⁷ | **2** | 10⁻⁷ to < 10⁻⁶ | Press brake, robot cell |
| **e** | 10⁻⁹ to < 10⁻⁸ | **3** | 10⁻⁸ to < 10⁻⁷ | Drive-by-wire, surgical robot |

> **Memory Mnemonic:** "**aBcDe**" → as you go **a→e**, PFH_d gets **10× safer** each step.

---

## 2.6 Emergency Stop Circuit — Architecture Analysis

The emergency stop (E-stop) must conform to **ISO 13850 (EN 60204-1 § 9.2.5.8)** with the following **mandatory attributes**:
- **Category 0** (immediate removal of power) or **Category 1** (controlled stop, then power removal)
- **Normally Closed (NC) contact** in series — positive opening per **IEC 60947-5-1 Annex K**
- **Two-channel (redundant)** wiring to detect a single fault
- **Manual reset** required (no auto-restart) to verify operator intent
- **Palm-button** (red mushroom on yellow background) per **ISO 13850**

> **Logic Block (Boolean Latch):** $E_{stop\_active} = \overline{Reset} \times (K_1 \land K_2)$ where $K_1, K_2$ are the two E-stop NC contacts. **Reset** must be a **separate, deliberate** operator action.

---

## 2.7 Interlock Devices — Engineering Logic

Two major types per **ISO 14119**:
1. **Mechanical Interlock** — Tongue/key must physically enter the switch; cannot be defeated by a paper clip (low coding level).
2. **Coded Magnetic / RFID Interlock** — Unique electronic signature per guard door; high coding level (prevents defeat, used in high-risk machinery).

**Logic:** When guard opens → switch K1 opens → safety relay K1 drops out → contactor K2 de-energizes → motor stops. Fault monitoring: if K1 shorted (welded closed), the safety relay cannot be reset → **Category 3 / 4** achieved.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Connector Current Derating (Multi-Pin)

**Problem:** A 25-pin D-sub connector is rated **5 A per contact** for a single contact at 25 °C. The connector bundle will carry 5 A continuously on **each of the 25 pins** in an enclosed housing at an ambient of 35 °C. Compute the **derated current per contact** per IEC 60512.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Apply multi-pin derating factor for $N_{ckt} > 8$:**

$$
k_D = 1 - 0.04 \times (N_{ckt} - 8)
$$

Substitute $N_{ckt} = 25$:

$$
k_D = 1 - 0.04 \times (25 - 8) = 1 - 0.04 \times 17 = 1 - 0.68 = 0.32
$$

**Step 2 — Derated current at reference temperature (25 °C):**

$$
I_{25} = I_{max} \times k_D = 5 \text{ A} \times 0.32 = 1.6 \text{ A per contact}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply temperature derating (IEC 60512-3, simplified):**

$$
I_{derated} = I_{25} \times \sqrt{\dfrac{85 \text{ °C} - T_{amb}}{85 \text{ °C} - 25 \text{ °C}}}
$$

Substitute $T_{amb} = 35$ °C:

$$
I_{derated} = 1.6 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{85 - 35}{60}} = 1.6 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{50}{60}} = 1.6 \times \sqrt{0.8333} = 1.6 \times 0.9129
$$

$$
\boxed{I_{derated} \approx 1.46 \text{ A per contact}}
$$

**Interpretation:** The 25-pin connector cannot be used at its 5 A label rating; the safe continuous current is **1.46 A**. If the load requires 5 A, choose a connector with **larger contact cross-section** (e.g., power D-sub, or Han® heavy-duty).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Coaxial Connector Impedance

**Problem:** A BNC connector uses polyethylene dielectric ($\varepsilon_r = 2.25$) with outer conductor inner diameter $D = 6.0$ mm and inner conductor outer diameter $d = 2.0$ mm. Determine the characteristic impedance.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Apply the standard coaxial formula:**

$$
Z_0 = \dfrac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \ln\left(\dfrac{D}{d}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the diameter ratio:**

$$
\dfrac{D}{d} = \dfrac{6.0}{2.0} = 3.0
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\ln(3.0)$:**

$$
\ln(3.0) \approx 1.0986
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute:**

$$
Z_0 = \dfrac{60}{\sqrt{2.25}} \times 1.0986 = \dfrac{60}{1.5} \times 1.0986 = 40 \times 1.0986
$$

$$
\boxed{Z_0 \approx 43.9 \text{ Ω}}
$$

**Note:** Pure 50 Ω requires $D/d \approx 3.5$ for $\varepsilon_r = 2.25$. A 43.9 Ω cable would cause an **SWR ≈ 1.14** (4 % mismatch loss) — usually acceptable, but tight applications (test instruments) require exactly 50 Ω.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Safety Stopping Distance (ISO 13855)

**Problem:** A human operator approaches a hydraulic press at **v = 1.5 m/s**. The press uses a **light curtain** (resolution 14 mm). The machine's stopping time $T_s$ (response + brake) is **250 ms**. Compute the minimum safety distance for the curtain from the hazard point. Use the **vertical approach** formula.

### Solution

**Step 1 — General formula (ISO 13855 for vertical approach, $\leq 30$ mm resolution):**

$$
S = (K \times T_s) + C
$$

Where:
- $K = 1.6$ m/s (approach speed for vertical approach, hand-to-hazard) — **note**: for whole-body approach into a danger zone, $K = 1.6$ m/s; for vertical approach into a light curtain $K = 2.0$ m/s per ISO 13855 Table 1.
- $T_s$ = stopping time in **seconds**
- $C$ = intrusion distance (depends on resolution): $C = 8 \times (d - 14)$ mm, with $d$ = resolution in mm. For $d = 14$ mm, $C = 0$.

**Step 2 — Convert $T_s$ to seconds:** $T_s = 250 \text{ ms} = 0.250$ s

**Step 3 — Substitute (using $K = 1.6$ m/s as a conservative estimate):**

$$
S = (1.6 \times 0.250) + 0 = 0.4 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Add OSHA/ISO 13855 § 6.2 minimum (100 mm) and round up to nearest 50 mm:**

$$
\boxed{S_{min} = 500 \text{ mm (i.e., 0.5 m)}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Interpretation:** The light curtain must be mounted at least **500 mm** from the pinch point. If this is not possible, the machine must be slowed or the operator must use a **two-hand control** instead.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Risk Score Calculation (ISO 12100)

**Problem:** A robotic welding cell has the following hazard profile:
- **Severity (S):** S2 (serious, amputation possible)
- **Frequency of exposure (F):** F2 (frequent, daily)
- **Possibility of avoidance (P):** P1 (impossible, robot moves faster than human reflex)

Determine the risk level and required reduction.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Apply risk score equation:**

$$
R = S \times F \times P
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
R = 2 \times 2 \times 1 = 4
$$

**Step 3 — Risk Level Mapping (per EN ISO 12100 Annex A, simplified 5×5 matrix):**

| R Value | Risk Level | Required Action |
|---|---|---|
| 0 – 2 | Low | No action |
| 3 – 4 | Medium | Reduce by design |
| 5 – 7 | High | Add safeguarding |
| 8 – 25 | Very High | **Stop operation, redesign** |

**Step 4 — Decision:**

$$
\boxed{R = 4 \rightarrow \text{Medium Risk, engineering safeguards mandatory (e.g., safety-rated interlocked door + light curtain, PL d)}}
$$

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Safety Distance for a Horizontal Light Curtain (Horizontal Approach, $H \le 1000$ mm)

**Problem:** A horizontal light curtain (resolution 30 mm) is mounted at **h = 300 mm** above the floor, with **length L = 1500 mm**, in a robot cell with approach speed $K = 1.6$ m/s and stop time $T_s = 350$ ms.

### Solution

**Step 1 — ISO 13855 formula for horizontal approach (depth-related):**

$$
S = (K \times T_s) + C_{RO}
$$

Where $C_{RO}$ (reach-over distance) is from **ISO 13855 Table 2**, depending on $h$ (height of light curtain above floor) and the height of the hazard $a$:

For $h = 300$ mm, $a = 1000$ mm (typical hazard height): $C_{RO} \approx 1200$ mm (refer to ISO 13855 Table 2).

**Step 2 — Convert $T_s$:** $T_s = 0.350$ s

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$
S = (1.6 \times 0.350) + 1.200 = 0.560 + 1.200 = 1.760 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Round up to nearest 50 mm:**

$$
\boxed{S_{min} = 1.80 \text{ m}}
$$

| Aspect | Connector Selection | Safety Engineering |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary standard** | IEC 60603, IEC 61076 | ISO 12100, ISO 13849-1 |
| **Goal** | Reliable signal/power transfer | Reduce risk to acceptable level |
| **Failure mode concern** | Open circuit, high resistance | Dangerous undetected failure |
| **Test method** | Continuity, hipot, IR | PFHd calculation, validation |
| **Metric** | Mating cycles, mΩ | PL, SIL, PFHd |
| **Common pitfall** | Undersized contact (derating) | PPE used as first defense |

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

> **Q: Define the term "Performance Level (PL)" as per ISO 13849-1 and list the five discrete levels in order of increasing safety integrity.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The **Performance Level (PL)** is a discrete level used to specify the ability of safety-related parts of control systems (SRP/CS) to perform a safety function under foreseeable conditions. PL is graded from **'a' (lowest)** to **'e' (highest)**, corresponding to discrete ranges of average probability of dangerous failure per hour (**PFH_d**).

**Five Levels (1.5 marks):**

| PL | PFH_d (per hour) |
|---|---|
| a | $10^{-5} \text{ to } < 10^{-4}$ |
| b | $10^{-6} \text{ to } < 10^{-5}$ |
| c | $10^{-7} \text{ to } < 10^{-6}$ |
| d | $10^{-8} \text{ to } < 10^{-7}$ |
| e | $10^{-9} \text{ to } < 10^{-8}$ |

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

> **Q: What is meant by "derating" of a connector contact? List the three primary derating factors used in connector selection.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Derating is the practice of **operating a connector below its maximum rated value** to ensure long-term reliability under real-world conditions. The rated current and voltage on a connector's datasheet are typically determined for a **single isolated contact at 25 °C sea level** — not for the multi-pin bundle in a real enclosure.

**Three primary derating factors (2 marks):**
1. **Multi-pin derating factor ($k_D$):** When many contacts are bundled, mutual heating reduces current capacity.
2. **Temperature derating factor:** Higher ambient temperatures reduce the allowable current; the rule of thumb is $I_{derated} \propto \sqrt{T_{max} - T_{amb}}$.
3. **Altitude derating factor:** Reduced air density at high altitude lowers the dielectric breakdown strength, so the working voltage must be reduced.

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** Per KTU 2024 scheme, Part B questions offer an internal choice. **You must answer EITHER A OR B.**

---

### ❑ Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 / Model Paper 2024]**

> **(a)** With the aid of a labeled block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a Safety-Related Control System (SRP/CS)** in a CNC machine tool. Identify each block and state the standard to which it complies. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 25-pin D-sub connector is rated at **3.5 A per contact** at 25 °C, single contact. Compute the **derated current per pin** when used in a multi-pin configuration with **all 25 pins energized at 45 °C ambient**. The upper limit temperature of the contact is **85 °C**. State the safety implication of the result. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for A(a) — 7 marks

The SRP/CS of a CNC machine tool typically consists of **three functional blocks** per ISO 13849-1:

1. **Input / Sensing Block (1.5 marks):**
   - **Emergency Stop (E-Stop)** push-button with two NC contacts per **ISO 13850 / IEC 60947-5-1**.
   - **Guard Door Interlock** with mechanical tongue or coded RFID per **ISO 14119**.
   - **Light Curtain** (Type 4 per **IEC 61496-1/-2**) for operator presence detection.
   - **Safety Mat** (4-wire) for floor zone monitoring per **ISO 13856-1**.

2. **Logic / Evaluation Block (1.5 marks):**
   - A **safety relay (Category 4, PL e)** such as Pilz PNOZ s4 or Sick FX3-XTIO.
   - Performs **cross-fault monitoring** — if one channel is shorted, the relay cannot be reset.
   - Provides **time-delay** for controlled stop (Category 1) per **IEC 60204-1 § 9.2.5.8**.

3. **Output / Actuation Block (1.5 marks):**
   - **Two contactors in series (K1 and K2)** with mechanically linked auxiliary contacts (mirror contacts) per **IEC 60947-4-1 Annex F**.
   - **STO (Safe Torque Off)** input to the CNC servo drive per **IEC 61800-5-2**.
   - **Power removal** of motor supply (Category 0) or controlled stop followed by removal (Category 1).

4. **Diagnostics and Reset (1 mark):**
   - **Manual reset** PB (separate from start PB).
   - **Fault indicator** (red lamp) and **OK indicator** (green lamp).
   - **Logbook** of safety events for ISO 12100 validation.

5. **Standards Converge** (1 mark):
   - The overall system must validate to **PL d** (CNC machines) or **PL e** (presses, robots) per **ISO 13849-1**.
   - The safety function must have **PFH_d < 10⁻⁷ /h** for PL d.

6. **Block Diagram** (0.5 marks) — as shown in **Section 4.1** of these notes.

#### Model Solution for A(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Multi-pin derating factor (1.5 marks):**

$$
k_D = 1 - 0.04 \times (N_{ckt} - 8) = 1 - 0.04 \times (25 - 8) = 1 - 0.68 = 0.32
$$

**Step 2 — Derated current at reference temperature (1.5 marks):**

$$
I_{25} = I_{max} \times k_D = 3.5 \text{ A} \times 0.32 = 1.12 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 3 — Temperature derating (2 marks):**

$$
I_{derated} = I_{25} \times \sqrt{\dfrac{T_{max} - T_{amb}}{T_{max} - 25 \text{ °C}}} = 1.12 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{85 - 45}{85 - 25}}
$$

$$
I_{derated} = 1.12 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{40}{60}} = 1.12 \times \sqrt{0.6667} = 1.12 \times 0.8165
$$

$$
\boxed{I_{derated} \approx 0.915 \text{ A per pin}}
$$

**Step 4 — Safety implication (2 marks):**

The 25-pin D-sub connector at full loading and 45 °C ambient can only carry **0.92 A per pin**, i.e., **only 26 % of its nameplate rating**. Exceeding this will cause:
- **Thermal runaway** of the contact, leading to oxidation and increased contact resistance.
- **Insulation degradation** (PBT housing softens above 130 °C but epoxy interfaces may fail earlier).
- **Voltage breakdown** at altitude if used in non-pressurized enclosures.
- **Safety implication:** A wiring harness mis-rated for current is a **fire hazard**; the panel builder must either:
  (a) Choose a connector with higher per-contact rating (e.g., Han® 6B at 16 A/pin), or
  (b) Distribute the current across multiple parallel pins.

---

### ❑ Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Model Paper]**

> **(a)** Explain the **three-step risk reduction methodology** defined in **ISO 12100:2010**, with one practical engineering example for each step, applied to an **industrial robot cell**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A vertical light curtain with **14 mm resolution** is installed in a packaging machine. The **stop time of the machine is 280 ms** and the **operator approach speed is 1.6 m/s**. Calculate the **minimum safety distance** for the light curtain. Comment on the validity of the installation if the available mounting distance is **0.45 m**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for B(a) — 7 marks

The ISO 12100:2010 three-step method (in order of priority) is:

**Step 1 — Inherent Safe Design (2.5 marks):**
- **Principle:** Remove the hazard or reduce risk by changing the machine itself, not by adding protective equipment.
- **Robot-cell example:**
  - Reduce the **dynamic energy** of the robot by limiting its maximum velocity from 3 m/s to 1.5 m/s.
  - Use **collaborative-rated (cobot) end effectors** with rounded edges, compliant padding, and force-limited joints.
  - Replace **hydraulic actuators** with **electric servos** to eliminate hydraulic fluid leak hazards.
  - Use **intrinsically safe (IS) circuits** in explosive powder-coating booths.

**Step 2 — Safeguarding (Engineering Controls) (2.5 marks):**
- **Principle:** Add protective devices between the hazard and the operator when Step 1 is insufficient.
- **Robot-cell example:**
  - **Light curtain (Type 4, IEC 61496)** at the cell boundary.
  - **Interlocked guard door with coded RFID (ISO 14119)** — access possible only when robot is in safe-stop.
  - **Area scanner (laser, SICK nanoScan3)** for mobile robot fleets.
  - **Two-hand control** for press operations (ISO 13851).

**Step 3 — Information for Use (1.5 marks):**
- **Principle:** Warn and train the user about residual risks that cannot be removed by Step 1 or Step 2.
- **Robot-cell example:**
  - **Warning labels** (ISO 7010: W014 "Robot in operation").
  - **Lockout-Tagout (LOTO) procedure** per **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.147** / **EN ISO 14118**.
  - **Operator training** on emergency-stop locations and PPE use.
  - **User manual** with residual-risk list per **EN 82079-1**.

(0.5 marks for the final summary statement that PPE is the *last* resort.)

#### Model Solution for B(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — ISO 13855 formula for vertical approach (1.5 marks):**

$$
S = (K \times T_s) + C
$$

Where:
- $K = 1.6$ m/s (per ISO 13855 Table 1, vertical approach into a light curtain)
- $T_s$ = stop time in **seconds** = 0.280 s
- $C = 8 \times (d - 14)$ mm, with $d$ = resolution in mm. For $d = 14$ mm, $C = 0$ mm.

**Step 2 — Substitute (1.5 marks):**

$$
S = (1.6 \times 0.280) + 0 = 0.448 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply ISO 13855 minimum distance (1.5 marks):**

ISO 13855 mandates a **minimum safety distance of 100 mm** even when the calculated value is lower. So:

$$
S_{calculated} = 0.448 \text{ m} \approx 448 \text{ mm}
$$

Since $448 \text{ mm} > 100 \text{ mm}$, the calculated value governs.

**Step 4 — Round up to nearest 50 mm (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{S_{min} = 450 \text{ mm} = 0.45 \text{ m}}
$$

**Step 5 — Validity comment (1.5 marks):**

The available mounting distance of **0.45 m** is **marginally acceptable** — it exactly matches the computed minimum. However, in practice this is **not acceptable** because:
- **No engineering tolerance is provided**; any degradation in stop time (e.g., brake wear, valve lag) would cause the safety distance to be insufficient.
- **A safety margin of at least 10–20 %** is recommended in industrial practice.
- **Recommendation:** Either (a) move the curtain to **≥ 0.5 m**, or (b) reduce $T_s$ by upgrading the brake system to ≤ 240 ms (which would give $S = 0.384$ m, allowing 0.45 m with 17 % margin).

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **PPE-Listed-First Syndrome (–2 marks):** Many students list PPE/face shield as the *first* safety measure. **Per ISO 12100, PPE is the LAST step.** Examiners immediately deduct marks for this.
>
> 2. **Confusing PL with SIL (–2 marks):** PL is per **ISO 13849-1** (uses PFH_d bands a–e); SIL is per **IEC 61508 / IEC 62061** (uses PFH_d bands 1–4). They are *related but not identical*. Mapping is approximate (PL d ≈ SIL 2), not exact.
>
> 3. **Forgetting Multi-Pin Derating (–1.5 marks):** A common computational mistake is to use the 5 A label rating directly. Always apply the **multi-pin derating factor** for $N_{ckt} > 8$ and the **temperature derating** for $T_{amb} > 25$ °C.
>
> 4. **Using Contact Resistance Formula with Wrong Units (–1 mark):** $R_c = \rho L / A$ gives Ohms in SI; convert to mΩ for connector datasheets.
>
> 5. **Omitting Units in the Final Answer (–0.5 mark):** Always state units (mΩ, °C, A, mm, m).
>
> 6. **Drawing the Light Curtain Between the Hazard and the Operator (–1 mark):** In safety diagrams, the **safeguard (light curtain) must be between the operator and the hazard**, not on the operator's side of the work envelope.
>
> 7. **Auto-Reset on E-Stop (–3 marks — major):** Emergency stops per **ISO 13850** must require a **separate, deliberate manual reset** to prevent automatic restart. Auto-restart is a textbook violation.
>
> 8. **Neglecting Stop Time Units (–1 mark):** When computing safety distance, $T_s$ must be in **seconds**, not milliseconds. Forgetting to divide by 1000 is a frequent error.
