## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📋 **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin This Page)**

### A. Connectors
- ☐ **Definition:** Passive electromechanical device providing a separable electrical connection.
- ☐ **Five sub-assemblies:** Contact, insulator, shell, strain relief, latch.
- ☐ **Contact materials:** Phosphor bronze, BeCu, brass with Au/Ni or Sn plating.
- ☐ **Multi-pin derating formula:** $k_D = 1 - 0.04 (N_{ckt} - 8)$ for $N_{ckt} > 8$.
- ☐ **Temperature derating formula:** $I_{derated} = I_{25} \sqrt{(T_{max} - T_{amb}) / (T_{max} - 25)}$.
- ☐ **Coax impedance formula:** $Z_0 = (60/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}) \ln(D/d)$. For 50 Ω, $D/d \approx 3.5$ in polyethylene.
- ☐ **Connector types to remember:** D-sub, M12, USB-C, RJ45, Han®, BNC, SMA, LC, SC, MPO, IDC, IEC 60320 (C13/C14).
- ☐ **Key parameters:** Mating cycles (100 → 50 000), contact resistance (< 30 mΩ), insulation resistance (> 1000 MΩ), DWV (1000 V AC).
- ☐ **IP rating ladder:** IP20 (indoor) → IP44 (splash) → IP67 (submersion 1 m) → IP69K (high-pressure hot washdown).

### B. Safety of Machinery
- ☐ **Three-step ISO 12100:** Inherent design → Safeguarding → Information for use (PPE last).
- ☐ **PL scale:** a b c d e (10× safer per step). PL d → PFH_d < 10⁻⁷ /h.
- ☐ **SIL scale:** 1 2 3 4. SIL 2 ↔ PL d (approximate).
- ☐ **Safety distance formula:** $S = K T_s + C$, with $K = 1.6$ m/s vertical, $2.0$ m/s whole-body.
- ☐ **E-Stop must be:** Two-channel, NC, manual reset, ISO 13850 mushroom button.
- ☐ **Interlock standard:** ISO 14119 (mechanical or coded RFID).
- ☐ **Light curtain standard:** IEC 61496-1/-2 (Type 4 for high-risk).
- ☐ **Two-hand control:** ISO 13851.
- ☐ **Lockout-Tagout:** OSHA 29 CFR 1910.147 / EN ISO 14118.
- ☐ **Risk score:** $R = S \times F \times P$ (each on 1–5 scale).
- ☐ **Validation always required:** Per ISO 13849-2 (testing of safety functions).

### C. Cross-Cutting Mnemonics
- ☐ **"I-PV-RV"** — Identify → Pinpoint PL → Validate → Re-Verify.
- ☐ **"aBcDe"** — PL levels with PFH_d 10⁻⁵ → 10⁻⁹.
- ☐ **"4-1-2-1"** — Pin count categories of most-used connectors: D-sub (9/15/25/37/50), M12 (3/4/5/8), USB (4/9/24), RJ (4/6/8).
- ☐ **"DT-A-IL"** — Connector derating: **D**istance, **T**emperature, **A**ltitude, **I**nter-pin, **L**ifetime.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Pro Tip:** Whenever a safety question appears, **always cite the standard number** (ISO 12100, ISO 13849-1, ISO 13850, IEC 61496, etc.) and **always close with a validation statement** ("the system must be validated by testing per ISO 13849-2"). This single habit can add 1–2 marks per answer.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Criteria and Related Test Methods for Service Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT411 - PERUT411
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Performance Criteria and Related Test Methods for Service Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (ISO 8373:2021)

A **Service Robot** is formally defined by the International Organization for Standardization (ISO 8373:2021) as:

> *"A robot that performs useful tasks for humans or equipment, excluding industrial automation applications."*

**Performance Criteria** are a set of **quantitative, measurable, and repeatable** metrics used to objectively evaluate the operational capability of a service robot under specified environmental and operational conditions. They act as a **universal language** between manufacturers, integrators, regulators, and end-users.

**Test Methods** are the **standardized experimental procedures** (test setups, trajectories, payloads, environmental conditions, and statistical analysis methods) used to measure those performance criteria in a reproducible way.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For the 2024 scheme, students must know that the foundational standards for service robot performance evaluation are the **ISO 18646 series** (for locomotion, navigation, and manipulation) and **ISO 13482:2014** (for personal care robots). These are the *only* universally accepted benchmarks referenced in KTU-endorsed robotics labs and industry internships.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Car Crash Test for Robots"

Imagine buying a vacuum cleaner. You do not just trust the advertisement; you look for a **star rating** (energy efficiency, noise level, suction power). 

Now imagine buying a **mobile service robot** (e.g., a hospital delivery robot, a floor-cleaning robot, or a humanoid assistant). How do you know it can climb a 10 mm threshold, travel 2 km on one charge, or pick up a 500 g cup without crushing it? 

**Performance criteria are the "star rating" of a robot**, and **test methods are the controlled laboratory experiments** that produce those ratings — exactly like the way **Euro NCAP** tests car safety or **BEE star labels** rate air conditioners in India. Without these, every company would publish its own "best in the world" claims, making comparison impossible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of Robot Performance Evaluation:**
> 1. **Repeatability** — Can the robot do the *same* task *the same way* repeatedly?
> 2. **Accuracy** — How *close* does the robot get to the desired target?
> 3. **Robustness** — Does it still work when conditions change (lighting, floor texture, payload)?

## 1.3 Why Performance Criteria Matter in KTU Context

In the KTU 2024 B.Tech curriculum, robotics labs evaluate student-built prototypes. Without standardized criteria, grading a line-following robot vs. a SLAM-based robot becomes subjective. The ISO framework provides:
- **CO1 (Engineering Knowledge)** alignment through mathematical metrics.
- **CO5 (Modern Tool Usage)** alignment through digital twin simulation of tests.
- A common language for **capstone projects and industry internships**.

**Key Standard Reference Values (Highlighted Constants):**
- **Standard Test Floor:** Smooth, level concrete or vinyl with friction coefficient **μ ≥ 0.6** (per ISO 18646-1).
- **Standard Ambient Conditions:** Temperature **20 °C ± 5 °C**, Humidity **45%–75%**.
- **Standard Reference Speed:** **v_ref = 0.5 m/s** for indoor wheeled service robots.
- **Standard Cube Dimension:** **500 mm × 500 mm × 500 mm** for obstacle avoidance tests.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The ISO 18646 Framework — Decomposed

The ISO 18646 series is the cornerstone of service robot performance testing. It is structured into four parts, each addressing a distinct capability:

### Part 1 — ISO 18646-1:2016 (Wheeled Robots — Locomotion)
Evaluates how well a **wheeled** mobile robot moves. Test categories:
1. **Straight-line driving on flat ground** → measures speed, deviation, slip.
2. **Curved path tracking** → measures understeer/oversteer behavior.
3. **Step climbing** → maximum step height (h_step) the robot can ascend/descend.
4. **Slope climbing** → maximum gradient angle (θ_slope) before tipping.
5. **Gap crossing** → maximum gap width (w_gap) between two platforms.
6. **Obstacle avoidance** → minimum clearance around static obstacles.

### Part 2 — ISO 18646-2:2019 (Legged Robots — Locomotion)
Specialized for **bipedal, quadrupedal, and hexapod** robots. Adds:
- **Static stability margin (SSM)**
- **Zero Moment Point (ZMP) deviation**
- **Energy cost of transport (CoT)**

### Part 3 — ISO 18646-3:2021 (Manipulators)
Evaluates **arm-type** service robot manipulation:
- **Pose accuracy (AP)** and **Pose repeatability (RP)** of end-effector.
- **Payload mass** vs. reach.
- **Drift under load** — how much the arm sags with payload.
- **Multi-point path execution** (linear and circular).

### Part 4 — ISO 18646-4:2021 (Supportive / Background Environment)
Defines the **test environment** — floor types, lighting, ambient noise, fiducial markers, and reference objects used in all other parts.

## 2.2 Logical Flow of a Performance Test (5-Step Standard)

A standardized KTU/industry test follows this exact pipeline:

1. **Test Setup Definition** — Floor, lighting, temperature, fiducial markers placed per ISO 18646-4.
2. **Calibration** — Robot self-calibrates using a known reference target (e.g., AprilTag of size 100 mm).
3. **Commanded Motion** — Robot executes a predefined path (e.g., 5 m straight line, then 90° turn, then return).
4. **Data Acquisition** — Internal sensors (wheel encoders, IMU) and external (motion capture like Vicon/OptiTrack) record actual trajectory.
5. **Statistical Analysis** — Mean error, standard deviation, max error, RMSE are computed across **N ≥ 30 trials**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table lists **all** high-yield formulas for solving numerical problems on this topic in KTU examinations.

| **#** | **Performance Metric** | **Mathematical Formula** | **Unit** | **Where It Applies (ISO)** |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | Pose Accuracy | $AP_x = \sqrt{(\bar{x} - x_c)^2 + (\bar{y} - y_c)^2 + (\bar{z} - z_c)^2}$ | m or mm | ISO 18646-3 |
| 2 | Pose Repeatability | $RP = \bar{l} + 3\,S_l$ where $l_i = \sqrt{(x_i - \bar{x})^2 + (y_i - \bar{y})^2 + (z_i - \bar{z})^2}$ | m or mm | ISO 18646-3 |
| 3 | Path Accuracy (Straight) | $PA = \max_{i} \vert d_i \vert$ where $d_i$ is lateral deviation | mm | ISO 18646-1 |
| 4 | Path Repeatability | $PR = \pm \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(d_i - \bar{d})^2}$ | mm | ISO 18646-1 |
| 5 | Static Stability Margin | $SSM = \frac{d_{CoM\text{-}to\text{-}edge}}{R_{support}}$ (dimensionless ratio) | unitless | ISO 18646-2 |
| 6 | Zero Moment Point Deviation | $\Delta_{ZMP} = \sqrt{(x_{ZMP} - x_{ref})^2 + (y_{ZMP} - y_{ref})^2}$ | m | ISO 18646-2 |
| 7 | Cost of Transport | $CoT = \frac{P_{mech}}{m g v}$ | unitless | ISO 18646-2 |
| 8 | Slope Climbing Limit | $\tan(\theta_{max}) = \mu_s - \frac{a}{g\cos\theta}$ | degrees | ISO 18646-1 |
| 9 | Minimum Turning Radius | $R_{min} = \frac{L}{\sin(\delta_{max})}$ | m | ISO 18646-1 |
| 10 | Slip Ratio | $\sigma = \frac{v_{wheel} - v_{actual}}{v_{actual}} \times 100\%$ | % | ISO 18646-1 |
| 11 | Obstacle Avoidance Latency | $\tau_{avoid} = t_{detect} + t_{plan} + t_{execute}$ | s | ISO 18646-1 |
| 12 | Manipulation Drift | $\delta_{drift} = \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2 + \Delta z^2}$ under load | mm | ISO 18646-3 |
| 13 | Path Velocity Accuracy | $PVA = \frac{\vert v_{actual} - v_{cmd} \vert}{v_{cmd}} \times 100\%$ | % | ISO 18646-1 |
| 14 | Repeatability of Path Velocity | $RPV = \pm \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum (v_i - \bar{v})^2}$ | m/s | ISO 18646-1 |
| 15 | Pose Stabilization Time | $t_s$ at 2% settling band | s | ISO 18646-1 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Substitution Rule for Tables:** In every formula above, the symbol `$\vert \cdot \vert$` denotes *absolute value* (or modulus). In KTU answer sheets, write `mod` or use the math notation correctly. **Never** substitute a vertical pipe `|` inside a markdown table cell — it breaks the column structure.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These metrics are used in:
- **Hospital logistics robots** (e.g., Aethon TUG) — measured using ISO 18646-1 for elevator navigation.
- **Domestic vacuum robots** (e.g., iRobot Roomba) — gap crossing and obstacle avoidance tested per ISO 18646-1.
- **Surgical & rehabilitation robots** (e.g., Cyberdyne HAL) — CoT and SSM evaluated per ISO 18646-2.
- **Industrial collaborative service arms** (e.g., Universal Robots UR series) — pose accuracy tested per ISO 18646-3.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Pose Accuracy and Pose Repeatability

These two are the **most frequently asked 14-mark derivations** in KTU ESE. We derive them rigorously here.

### 3.1.1 Setup

Suppose a robot arm is commanded to reach a target point $P_c$ with coordinates $(x_c, y_c, z_c)$ in the world frame. Due to mechanical backlash, sensor noise, and control error, the actual end-effector reaches a slightly different point on every trial.

Let the **i-th** actual position be $P_i = (x_i, y_i, z_i)$ for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, N$ trials.

### 3.1.2 Mean Position (Centroid of Actual Reaches)

The best estimate of "where the robot actually goes" on average is the centroid:

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i, \quad
\bar{y} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i, \quad
\bar{z} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} z_i
$$

> **Logic:** This is the unbiased estimator of the mean under the Central Limit Theorem, which is valid because sensor noise is assumed Gaussian.

### 3.1.3 Pose Accuracy (AP)

**Definition:** Distance from the commanded point to the centroid of the actual reaches.

$$
\begin{aligned}
AP &= \sqrt{(\bar{x} - x_c)^2 + (\bar{y} - y_c)^2 + (\bar{z} - z_c)^2} \\[4pt]
   &= \sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2 + (\Delta z)^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** This is the 3-D Euclidean norm. If AP is large, the robot is *systematically biased* — calibration or mechanical alignment is wrong.

### 3.1.4 Distance of Each Trial from the Centroid

Define $l_i$ as the deviation of the i-th actual point from the centroid:

$$
l_i = \sqrt{(x_i - \bar{x})^2 + (y_i - \bar{y})^2 + (z_i - \bar{z})^2}
$$

### 3.1.5 Mean Deviation

$$
\bar{l} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} l_i
$$

### 3.1.6 Standard Deviation of Deviations

$$
S_l = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (l_i - \bar{l})^2}
$$

> **Logic:** Bessel's correction (N-1 in denominator) is used because the centroid $\bar{l}$ is itself estimated from the same data.

### 3.1.7 Pose Repeatability (RP)

ISO defines repeatability as the radius of a sphere that contains **99.7%** of all reaches (3-sigma confidence):

$$
RP = \bar{l} + 3 S_l
$$

> **Logic:** The factor 3 comes from the Gaussian distribution — 99.73% of values lie within ±3σ of the mean.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Full Solution)

**Problem:** A 6-DOF service robot arm is commanded to reach point $P_c = (500, 300, 200)$ mm. Over **N = 5** trials, the actual end-effector positions are recorded as:

| Trial $i$ | $x_i$ (mm) | $y_i$ (mm) | $z_i$ (mm) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 502 | 299 | 201 |
| 2 | 501 | 301 | 199 |
| 3 | 503 | 298 | 200 |
| 4 | 500 | 300 | 202 |
| 5 | 499 | 302 | 198 |

**Find:** (a) Pose Accuracy AP, (b) Pose Repeatability RP.

### Step 1: Compute the centroid

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{502+501+503+500+499}{5} = \frac{2505}{5} = 501.0 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\bar{y} = \frac{299+301+298+300+302}{5} = \frac{1500}{5} = 300.0 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\bar{z} = \frac{201+199+200+202+198}{5} = \frac{1000}{5} = 200.0 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 2: Compute AP

$$
\begin{aligned}
AP &= \sqrt{(501.0 - 500)^2 + (300.0 - 300)^2 + (200.0 - 200)^2} \\[4pt]
   &= \sqrt{(1.0)^2 + (0)^2 + (0)^2} = 1.0 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Compute $l_i$ for each trial

$$
l_1 = \sqrt{(502-501)^2 + (299-300)^2 + (201-200)^2} = \sqrt{1+1+1} = \sqrt{3} \approx 1.732 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
l_2 = \sqrt{(501-501)^2 + (301-300)^2 + (199-200)^2} = \sqrt{0+1+1} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
l_3 = \sqrt{(503-501)^2 + (298-300)^2 + (200-200)^2} = \sqrt{4+4+0} = \sqrt{8} \approx 2.828 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
l_4 = \sqrt{(500-501)^2 + (300-300)^2 + (202-200)^2} = \sqrt{1+0+4} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
l_5 = \sqrt{(499-501)^2 + (302-300)^2 + (198-200)^2} = \sqrt{4+4+4} = \sqrt{12} \approx 3.464 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 4: Compute $\bar{l}$

$$
\bar{l} = \frac{1.732 + 1.414 + 2.828 + 2.236 + 3.464}{5} = \frac{11.674}{5} = 2.335 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 5: Compute deviations $(l_i - \bar{l})$ and their squares

| $i$ | $l_i$ | $l_i - \bar{l}$ | $(l_i - \bar{l})^2$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 1.732 | -0.603 | 0.3636 |
| 2 | 1.414 | -0.921 | 0.8482 |
| 3 | 2.828 | 0.493 | 0.2430 |
| 4 | 2.236 | -0.099 | 0.0098 |
| 5 | 3.464 | 1.129 | 1.2746 |

### Step 6: Sum of squared deviations

$$
\sum (l_i - \bar{l})^2 = 0.3636 + 0.8482 + 0.2430 + 0.0098 + 1.2746 = 2.7392
$$

### Step 7: Compute $S_l$

$$
S_l = \sqrt{\frac{2.7392}{5-1}} = \sqrt{\frac{2.7392}{4}} = \sqrt{0.6848} = 0.8275 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 8: Compute RP

$$
RP = \bar{l} + 3 S_l = 2.335 + 3(0.8275) = 2.335 + 2.4825 = 4.818 \text{ mm}
$$

### Final Answer

$$
\boxed{AP = 1.0 \text{ mm}, \quad RP = 4.82 \text{ mm}}
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** The robot has a small systematic bias of 1 mm (can be corrected by calibration), but the spread of reaches (RP = 4.82 mm) is much larger, indicating **random noise** — likely from sensor quantization or backlash. Both must be reduced to meet industrial assembly tolerance (~0.1 mm).

## 3.3 Derivation: Cost of Transport (CoT) for Legged Robots

**CoT** measures the energy efficiency of locomotion — a critical metric for **battery-powered** service robots.

**Definition:** Mechanical power delivered to the robot's motors per unit weight, per unit speed.

$$
CoT = \frac{P_{mech}}{m g v}
$$

**Where:**
- $P_{mech}$ = total mechanical power output of all actuators (W)
- $m$ = total mass of robot (kg)
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration, **9.81 m/s²**
- $v$ = forward velocity (m/s)

**Derivation Steps:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Define mechanical work over distance } d: \quad W &= F \cdot d \\
\text{Step 2: Mechanical power: } \quad P_{mech} &= \frac{W}{t} = F v \\
\text{Step 3: Weight of robot: } \quad W_{weight} &= m g \\
\text{Step 4: Normalize power by weight and velocity: } \quad CoT &= \frac{P_{mech}}{m g v} = \frac{F v}{m g v} = \frac{F}{m g}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Insight:** CoT is *dimensionless*. A perfect theoretical minimum is CoT = 0.20 for walking bipeds. Real humanoid robots like Honda ASIMO achieve CoT ≈ 1.6; quadrupeds like Boston Dynamics Spot achieve CoT ≈ 0.5. *Lower is better.*

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Metric

| **Robot Type** | **Primary Metrics** | **Secondary Metrics** | **Applicable ISO Part** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Wheeled delivery robot (hospital) | Path accuracy, slip ratio | Battery endurance, obstacle clearance | ISO 18646-1 |
| Quadruped inspection robot | SSM, CoT, slope limit | Step climbing, gait symmetry | ISO 18646-2 |
| Biped humanoid assistant | ZMP deviation, CoT | Stabilization time, fall recovery | ISO 18646-2 |
| Service robot arm (kitchen helper) | AP, RP, payload drift | Reach envelope, joint range | ISO 18646-3 |
| Cleaning robot (domestic) | Obstacle avoidance latency | Coverage area, edge detection | ISO 18646-1 |
| Personal care robot (ISO 13482) | Safety-rated force limit | Contact detection time | ISO 13482:2014 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a service robot. List any four performance criteria specified in ISO 18646-1 for wheeled mobile robots.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A service robot (per ISO 8373:2021) is a robot that performs useful tasks for humans or equipment, excluding industrial automation applications. 
Four performance criteria for wheeled robots per ISO 18646-1:
1. **Straight-line driving accuracy** (lateral deviation).
2. **Slope climbing capability** (maximum gradient angle).
3. **Step climbing capability** (maximum step height).
4. **Obstacle avoidance performance** (minimum clearance and latency).

*(1 mark for definition + ½ mark each for four criteria = 3 marks)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the difference between Pose Accuracy (AP) and Pose Repeatability (RP)? Why is N ≥ 30 trials recommended?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **AP** is the distance between the *commanded* pose and the *centroid* of actual reached poses (systematic error).
- **RP** is the radius of the sphere containing ~99.7% of all reached poses, computed as $\bar{l} + 3S_l$ (random error/spread).
- **N ≥ 30 trials** is recommended because of the **Central Limit Theorem** — the sample mean of ≥30 independent trials approximates a Gaussian distribution, enabling valid 3-sigma statistical inference.

*(1 mark each for AP definition, RP definition, CLT justification = 3 marks)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the mathematical expressions for **Pose Accuracy (AP)** and **Pose Repeatability (RP)** of a service robot manipulator, clearly stating the meaning of each term used. Assume N trials with positions $(x_i, y_i, z_i)$ and a commanded position $(x_c, y_c, z_c)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wheeled service robot is tested on a 10 m straight track per ISO 18646-1. The lateral deviation $d_i$ (in mm) at 5 checkpoints over 6 trials is tabulated below. Compute (i) Mean Deviation, (ii) Path Accuracy (PA), (iii) Path Repeatability (PR), and (iv) comment on whether the robot is suitable for a hospital corridor application (tolerance ± 25 mm). **(7 marks)**

| Trial $\backslash$ Checkpoint | CP1 | CP2 | CP3 | CP4 | CP5 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| T1 | 5 | 8 | 12 | 10 | 6 |
| T2 | 4 | 9 | 11 | 9 | 7 |
| T3 | 6 | 7 | 13 | 11 | 5 |
| T4 | 5 | 8 | 12 | 10 | 6 |
| T5 | 4 | 10 | 11 | 9 | 8 |
| T6 | 5 | 9 | 12 | 10 | 7 |

#### Model Solution A(a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Given:** Commanded pose $P_c = (x_c, y_c, z_c)$. Actual i-th pose $P_i = (x_i, y_i, z_i)$, for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, N$.

**Step 1:** Centroid of actual reaches:

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i, \quad \bar{y} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i, \quad \bar{z} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} z_i
$$

**Step 2:** Pose Accuracy (systematic error):

$$
\begin{aligned}
AP &= \sqrt{(\bar{x} - x_c)^2 + (\bar{y} - y_c)^2 + (\bar{z} - z_c)^2} \\
   &= \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2 + \Delta z^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Deviation of i-th trial from centroid:

$$
l_i = \sqrt{(x_i - \bar{x})^2 + (y_i - \bar{y})^2 + (z_i - \bar{z})^2}
$$

**Step 4:** Mean deviation and standard deviation:

$$
\bar{l} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} l_i, \quad S_l = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N} (l_i - \bar{l})^2}
$$

**Step 5:** Pose Repeatability (3-sigma bound):

$$
RP = \bar{l} + 3 S_l
$$

*(1 mark for each step logically explained; remaining 2 marks for correct identification of variables and physical meaning.)*

#### Model Solution A(b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Step 1: Compute mean deviation at each checkpoint**

$$
\bar{d}_{CP1} = \frac{5+4+6+5+4+5}{6} = 4.83 \text{ mm}
$$
$$
\bar{d}_{CP2} = \frac{8+9+7+8+10+9}{6} = 8.50 \text{ mm}
$$
$$
\bar{d}_{CP3} = \frac{12+11+13+12+11+12}{6} = 11.83 \text{ mm}
$$
$$
\bar{d}_{CP4} = \frac{10+9+11+10+9+10}{6} = 9.83 \text{ mm}
$$
$$
\bar{d}_{CP5} = \frac{6+7+5+6+8+7}{6} = 6.50 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2: Path Accuracy (max lateral deviation)**

$$
PA = \max(\bar{d}) = 11.83 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(at CP3)}
$$

**Step 3: Path Repeatability** — compute the max standard deviation across checkpoints. For CP3:

$$
S_{CP3} = \sqrt{\frac{(12-11.83)^2+(11-11.83)^2+(13-11.83)^2+(12-11.83)^2+(11-11.83)^2+(12-11.83)^2}{5}}
$$

$$
S_{CP3} = \sqrt{\frac{0.029+0.689+1.369+0.029+0.689+0.029}{5}} = \sqrt{\frac{2.833}{5}} = \sqrt{0.567} = 0.753 \text{ mm}
$$

Similarly $S_{CP1}=0.753$, $S_{CP2}=0.957$, $S_{CP4}=0.753$, $S_{CP5}=0.957$.

Maximum $S = 0.957$ mm (at CP2 and CP5).

**Step 4: Path Repeatability**

$$
PR = \pm 3 \times 0.957 = \pm 2.87 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5: Suitability comment**
Total worst-case deviation = $PA + 3S = 11.83 + 2.87 = 14.70 \text{ mm}$, which is **within** ±25 mm tolerance. The robot is **suitable** for the hospital corridor application.

*(Marks: 1 for each mean + 1 for PA + 2 for std dev + 1 for PR + 2 for suitability comment = 7 marks)*

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **Cost of Transport (CoT)** metric used in ISO 18646-2 for legged service robots. Derive its expression and discuss why it is dimensionless. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A quadruped service robot of mass **m = 30 kg** moves at velocity **v = 0.6 m/s** on flat ground. The total mechanical power consumed by all 12 motors is **P = 180 W**. Compute (i) CoT, (ii) the range on a fully charged battery of capacity **E = 720 Wh**, and (iii) the energy consumed per kilometer of travel. Take g = 9.81 m/s². **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution B(a) — Theory + Derivation (7 marks)

**Definition:** Cost of Transport (CoT) is the mechanical energy required to move a robot's *unit weight* over *unit distance*. It is a universal benchmark for comparing the energy efficiency of different locomotion strategies (wheels vs. legs vs. hybrids).

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Mechanical work done over distance $d$ is $W = F \cdot d$, where $F$ is the total propulsive force (N).

**Step 2:** Mechanical power is $P_{mech} = F \cdot v$, where $v$ is forward velocity.

**Step 3:** Weight of the robot is $W_{robot} = m g$.

**Step 4:** Distance traveled in time $t$ is $d = v t$, so work per unit weight per unit distance is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CoT &= \frac{W / d}{m g} = \frac{F \cdot d / d}{m g} = \frac{F}{m g} \\
\text{Equivalently: } \quad CoT &= \frac{P_{mech}}{m g v}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why dimensionless:**
- Numerator: $[P_{mech}] = W = J/s = kg \cdot m^2 / s^3$
- Denominator: $[m g v] = kg \cdot (m/s^2) \cdot (m/s) = kg \cdot m^2 / s^3$
- Both sides have identical units of $kg \cdot m^2 / s^3$, so the ratio is **dimensionless**.

*(1 mark definition + 2 marks derivation + 2 marks dimensionless proof + 2 marks discussion of typical values like Spot CoT ≈ 0.5 and ASIMO CoT ≈ 1.6)*

#### Model Solution B(b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:** m = 30 kg, v = 0.6 m/s, P = 180 W, E = 720 Wh, g = 9.81 m/s²

**Step (i): Compute CoT**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CoT &= \frac{P}{m g v} = \frac{180}{30 \times 9.81 \times 0.6} \\
    &= \frac{180}{176.58} = 1.019
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{CoT \approx 1.02}
$$

**Step (ii): Compute range on 720 Wh battery**

Battery endurance time:

$$
t = \frac{E}{P} = \frac{720 \text{ Wh}}{180 \text{ W}} = 4.0 \text{ hours} = 14400 \text{ s}
$$

Range:

$$
R = v \times t = 0.6 \text{ m/s} \times 14400 \text{ s} = 8640 \text{ m} = 8.64 \text{ km}
$$

$$
\boxed{R = 8.64 \text{ km}}
$$

**Step (iii): Energy per kilometer**

$$
\frac{E}{R} = \frac{720 \text{ Wh}}{8.64 \text{ km}} = 83.33 \text{ Wh/km}
$$

$$
\boxed{83.33 \text{ Wh/km}}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** A CoT of 1.02 is moderate — typical for a quadruped at walking pace. Wheels would yield CoT ≈ 0.05. To improve, the robot could use **dynamic gait** (trot) which can reduce CoT to ~0.5.

*(2 marks for CoT + 2 marks for range + 1.5 marks for energy per km + 1.5 marks for engineering insight)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting Bessel's correction (N-1):** Students often divide by N instead of N-1 when computing $S_l$ — *this costs 1 full mark*. Always write: "$S_l = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum(l_i - \bar{l})^2}$".
> 2. **Confusing AP and RP:** AP is about the *centroid* vs. *command*; RP is about the *spread* of actual reaches. Examiners check this distinction strictly.
> 3. **Mixing up PA vs PR formulas:** Path Accuracy is the **maximum** deviation, not the mean. Path Repeatability uses the **standard deviation**, not the range.
> 4. **Units mismatch in CoT:** Some students forget to convert Wh → J or km → m. Always show unit conversions in steps.
> 5. **Skipping the dimensionless proof:** The KTU answer key explicitly awards marks for the unit-cancellation argument in CoT derivations.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Quick-Revision Checklist (High-Yield Bullet Summary):**

- **Service Robot Definition** (ISO 8373): Performs useful tasks for humans/equipment, *excluding* industrial automation.
- **ISO 18646-1:** Wheeled robot locomotion (speed, slope, step, gap, obstacle).
- **ISO 18646-2:** Legged robot locomotion (SSM, ZMP, CoT).
- **ISO 18646-3:** Manipulator performance (AP, RP, payload, drift).
- **ISO 18646-4:** Test environment (floor, lighting, fiducials).
- **ISO 13482:** Personal care robot safety standards.
- **Pose Accuracy:** $AP = \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2 + \Delta z^2}$ (centroid vs. command).
- **Pose Repeatability:** $RP = \bar{l} + 3S_l$ (3-sigma bound on trial spread).
- **Path Accuracy:** $PA = \max \vert d_i \vert$ (max lateral deviation).
- **Path Repeatability:** $PR = \pm 3 S_d$ (3-sigma of deviation).
- **Cost of Transport:** $CoT = P / (m g v)$ — dimensionless energy efficiency.
- **Static Stability Margin:** $SSM = d_{CoM\text{-}to\text{-}edge} / R_{support}$.
- **ZMP Deviation:** Distance between actual and reference ZMP.
- **Slope Limit:** $\tan(\theta_{max}) = \mu_s - a/(g\cos\theta)$ — must hold before tipping.
- **Test Trials:** Always N ≥ 30 to invoke Central Limit Theorem and 3-sigma statistics.
- **Bessel's Correction:** Divide by (N-1) when computing sample standard deviation.
- **Test Cell:** 20 °C ± 5 °C, 45–75% RH, μ ≥ 0.6 floor, 500 lux uniform lighting.
- **Reference Speed:** 0.5 m/s for indoor wheeled service robots.
- **Reference Cube:** 500 mm for obstacle avoidance tests.
- **Dimensional Check:** CoT derivation is a favorite 14-mark question — always show the unit cancellation.
- **Industry Examples:** Spot (CoT ≈ 0.5), ASIMO (CoT ≈ 1.6), Roomba (uses ISO 18646-1 gap/obstacle tests).
- **Real-World Use:** Hospitals (TUG), homes (Roomba), inspection (ANYmal), personal care (PARO, Pepper).
- **Examiner Trap:** AP ≠ RP — AP is *systematic*, RP is *random*. Always state which.
- **Examiner Trap 2:** $l_i$ uses the *centroid* (not the commanded point) — using the command gives Path Accuracy, not Repeatability.
